channel.title: George & the Word
channel.description: Weekly notes on faith, work, church, marriage, and life lived under His word.

item.title: Nobody Is Self-Made
item.description: A box of old photographs sits open on a kitchen table, curling at the corners, black and white, a boy of nine standing barefoot beside a plow horse twice his size. Behind that single image are the ones nobody keeps in albums: an uncle who taught the welding, a neighbor who cosigned a loan the bank refused, a teacher who quietly paid an exam fee and never mentioned it again. And yet the story usually told about that boy, grown now and successful, is the story of grit alone, of nobody handing him anything. It is not a lie exactly. It is simply the version with the ground edited out.

item.title: Busy Isn't Faithful
item.description: I've read the story of Mary and Martha more times than I could count, always slightly annoyed at how neatly it seems to resolve, Martha distracted with much serving, Mary sitting at the Lord's feet, and Jesus telling Martha she is anxious and troubled about many things when only one thing is necessary. For years I heard that as a story about two personality types, the doer and the contemplative, and assumed I was simply built as a Martha and would have to make my peace with it. What I didn't notice until that kitchen, laminating machine humming, was that Jesus never rebukes the serving itself. He names the anxiety underneath it, the many things standing in for the one thing, and I recognized that anxiety immediately because it was mine, dressed up as diligence, running on a machine at nine at night for a task that could have waited until morning or been handed to someone else entirely.

item.title: What Poverty Taught Me About Riches
item.description: I was counting coins on my kitchen table again, arranging them into small silver towers to see if they'd stretch to cover both the rent balance and a bag of maize flour, and they wouldn't, not by a wide enough margin to matter. My phone buzzed with a mobile money request from my brother, and I stared at it the way you stare at a wound you can't afford to treat, then set the phone face down and kept stacking coins that had already been counted twice. Somewhere outside a neighbor's radio was playing a gospel song about God's abundant blessings, and I remember feeling something close to irritation at it, the way you can feel irritated at a joke that isn't landing because it's too close to your actual life.

item.title: Faithfulness Can Feel Lonely
item.description: I was the last car in the church parking lot again, the one who stays to stack chairs and lock the side door, and as I pulled out onto the empty street I caught myself doing the math I try not to do, counting how many people had actually shown up to the thing I'd spent three weeks organizing. Six. Six people, after all those emails and all that praying over the sign-up sheet, and I sat at the red light replaying every reason I could have skipped it myself, wondering if anyone would have noticed either way. It wasn't dramatic, this feeling. It was just tired and small and quiet, the kind of discouragement that doesn't announce itself so much as settle into the seat beside you.

item.title: Churches Don't Drift Toward Truth
item.description: I was on the church budget committee the year we voted to cut the visitation ministry, and I remember sitting in that fluorescent-lit fellowship hall telling myself it was practical, just numbers, just a program that wasn't drawing enough volunteers anymore. Nobody in that room decided to stop caring about the homebound and the grieving. We just decided, one reasonable line item at a time, over several years, to stop funding the structures that made caring for them possible. I drove home that night with an unease I couldn't quite name, the sense that something had shifted underneath us without anyone choosing it.

item.title: Waiting Is Not Wasting
item.description: I was folding the same load of laundry for the third time, not because it needed refolding but because my hands needed something to do while the two pink lines refused to become the two pink lines I wanted them to be, month after month, for going on two years now. My sister-in-law had just announced her second, and I'd smiled and hugged her in the driveway and then gone inside and folded towels that were already folded, because if I sat still I was going to have to feel the whole shape of what waiting had become in my life, which by then felt less like a season and more like a sentence.

item.title: Terrified of Silence
item.description: I turned my phone over so many times that night I lost count, checking for a message that wasn't coming, from a company that had already made its decision three business days ago and simply hadn't bothered to tell me. The apartment was quiet in the way it gets after the second unemployment week, when you've stopped even turning on the television for noise because the noise started to feel worse than the silence. I remember standing at the kitchen counter at nearly midnight, not praying exactly, just standing there, waiting for something, anything, to press back against the quiet.

item.title: Whatever You Do, As Unto the Lord
item.description: It was 3 a.m. on the line, the fourth night shift in a row, and I was running the same bolt into the same hole for the six hundredth time that night, thinking about how nobody would ever know or care whether I did it well or badly, so long as it passed inspection. The radio was playing something forgettable. My back hurt in the specific way it hurts after year six of this job. I remember wondering, not for the first time, whether this was what my life had come down to, whether there was any difference between doing the work carefully and doing it carelessly, since either way the truck rolled off the line and someone drove away in it without a thought for my hands.

item.title: You Are Not Your Job Title
item.description: I sat in my car in the parking garage for twenty minutes after the meeting, engine off, badge already collected, a cardboard box of picture frames and a coffee mug on the passenger seat like evidence from a crime scene. Fifteen years, and the last conversation lasted six minutes. Somewhere in there I remember thinking, absurdly, that I didn't know how to introduce myself anymore at a dinner party, because the first thing people ask is what you do, and I no longer had an answer that fit on a business card.

item.title: The Bible Was Never About You
item.description: And that is the best news you will ever receive.

item.title: The Respectable Sin Nobody Talks About
item.description: We have graded evil. God has not.

item.title: Why Friendship Feels So Rare
item.description: Two are better than one. So where did everyone go?

item.title: Work Existed Before Sin
item.description: A letter to the person who feels guilty for loving what they do.

item.title: The Verse That Is Slowly Killing You
item.description: You did not misquote it. You just ripped it out of the ground.

item.title: God Doesn't Need Your Resume
item.description: He called the unqualified. He still does.

item.title: The Jesus You Missed in the Old Testament
item.description: He was never hiding. We just were not looking.

item.title: Your Mind Is Under Attack — And You Don’t Even See It
item.description: The most dangerous wars are those where the occupied believe they are still free.

item.title: You Were Not Made for Sadness
item.description: What It Means to Guard Joy in the Lord
